coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric and rapid-response fluorescent nanoprobe (NA-CQDs) for the detection of hypochlorous acid based on naphthalimide (NA) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been prepared. The nanoprobe NA-CQDs showed blue fluorescence with a unique yellow fluorescence in the presence of HClO, accompanying the emission wavelength change from 457 nm to 538 nm. The fluorescence detection limit for HClO was 99 nM with a linear range from 0.1 to 32 μM. The nanoprobe exhibited a rapid response to HClO within 20 s. The sensing mechanism might be attributed to the oxidation and cleavage of p-aminophenylether group on the nanoprobe NA-CQDs, leading to the FRET process from the CQDs to the NA due to enhanced ICT of the NA portion. A series of real applications were conducted including spiked real water samples, test paper strips, and fluorescence bioimaging in RAW 264.7 and HepG2 cells and nude mice, highlighting the potential application of NA-CQDs for future environmental sensing and biosensing in living things.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric and rapid-response fluorescent nanoprobe based on modification of carbon quantum dots by naphthalimide for HClO bioimaging in living cells and mice

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Separation Membrane Materials,College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Separation Membrane Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Separation Membrane Materials,College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric and rapid-response fluorescent nanoprobe (NA-CQDs) for the detection of hypochlorous acid based on naphthalimide (NA) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been prepared. The nanoprobe NA-CQDs showed blue fluorescence with a unique yellow fluorescence in the presence of HClO, accompanying the emission wavelength change from 457 nm to 538 nm. The fluorescence detection limit for HClO was 99 nM with a linear range from 0.1 to 32 μM. The nanoprobe exhibited a rapid response to HClO within 20 s. The sensing mechanism might be attributed to the oxidation and cleavage of p-aminophenylether group on the nanoprobe NA-CQDs, leading to the FRET process from the CQDs to the NA due to enhanced ICT of the NA portion. A series of real applications were conducted including spiked real water samples, test paper strips, and fluorescence bioimaging in RAW 264.7 and HepG2 cells and nude mice, highlighting the potential application of NA-CQDs for future environmental sensing and biosensing in living things.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorous acid (HClO) has been widely used as an important component of bleaches and disinfectants in daily life [1]. In addition to the external environment, it can be produced in living organisms from H2O2 in the presence of chloride and myeloperoxidase (MPO) [2]. Endogenous HClO can act as an antibacterial agent in the innate immune system to destroy invasive bacteria [3]. Therefore, HClO levels can serve as a detection indicator to determine the situation inside the organism. However, due to its strong oxidizing properties, abnormal levels of HClO can damage some components within the cells [4,5], which may result in tissue damage and lead to many diseases, such as cardiovascular disease [6], lung injury [7], and neurodegenerative disorders [8,9]. It is therefore important to establish a simple and convenient method for monitoring HClO in real water and biological samples.

Compared to electrochemical and UV–vis colorimetric methods, fluorescent probes have been widely developed in the detection field because of their high sensitivity, non-invasiveness, and easy operation [10–14], such as small molecule fluorophores [15–17], metallic nanoclusters [18–20], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [21–23]. Especially, CQDs have attracted considerable attention as nanoprobes in the field of fluorescence sensing and bioimaging due to their good water solubility, exceptional fluorescence properties, ease of preparation, abundant raw materials, environmental friendliness, and low toxicity [24–26]. Wu et al. synthesized a green fluorescent nanoprobe (G-CQDs) to successfully detect exogenous HClO in living cells based on the fluorescent quenching mechanism [27]. Pang et al. have also used the quenching mechanism to design a bi-functional near-infrared fluorescent CQDs probe for HClO with the detection limit of 42.7 nM, which was used in water samples and cell membrane localization function [28]. Wu et al. successfully prepared the red-emissive turn-off CQDs, which was applied in subcellular localization and cellular imaging [29]. Different from the above quenching fluorescent nanoprobe, Li et al. reported a nitrogen-doped CQDs turn-on nanoprobe based on aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) for HClO in the range of 0.01 to 1 mM [30]. Such turn-on probes have the advantage of increasing the signal-to-background noise ratio and widening linear range, which would have better quantification and imaging prospects in actual practice.

Compared to turn-off and turn-on nanoprobes, ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes have been the focus of attention due to their built-in correction function, which could avoid interference from the instrument efficiency, the measurement conditions, and the concentration of sensor or probe, especially in living organisms, such as living cells and mice [31]. Several studies have been carried out based on multiple-emission CQDs or the composites of CQDs with other materials to design ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on CQDs [32,33]. In the first method, Zhang et al. constructed a blue and green emission CQDs-based probe by hydrothermal treatment of rose bengal, sodium citrate, and triethylenetetramine for HClO detection in tap water [34]; Tang et al. synthesized blue-emission CQDs by the one-step solvothermal treatment of hydroquinone and 5-aminoisphthalic acid, which displayed ratiometric (blue to green) fluorescence for visual detection of HClO in test strips [35]. In the second method, Zhong et al. presented a nanoprobe for the rapid ratiometric fluorescence detection of HClO, which weakened inner filter effect (IFE) of curcumin (CCM) on CQDs based on the oxidation of CCM by HClO [36]; Cheng et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (GSH@Si-CQDs) based on glutathione (GSH) modified Si-CQDs, which was quenched at 430 nm and enhanced at 580 nm in the presence of HClO [37]; Yan et al. prepared a ratiometric nanoprobe based on fluorescein-modified CQDs (FH-GA-CQDs) through amide bond linkage using fluorescein and GA-CQDs, which was successfully applied for vivo bioimaging of HClO in living cells and nude mice [31].

In this work, a ratiometric nanoprobe (NA-CQDs) based on the composites of CQDs with small molecule fluorophore’s derivative was prepared by modifying 4-(4-aminophenoxy)-N-amino-1,8-naphthalimide (NA) onto CQDs to obtain the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) platform, where the CQDs was used as energy donors and a naphthalimide fluorophore was used as an energy acceptor to obtain the blue-to-yellow ratiometric HClO nanoprobe. The nanoprobe NA-CQDs was characterized by 1H NMR, FT-IR, XPS, zeta potential, UV–vis, and TEM. The sensing mechanism was also investigated. Finally, the real applications of spiked real water samples, test paper strips, and fluorescence bioimaging in RAW 264.7 and HepG2 cells and nude mice were explored.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) and ethylenediamine monohydrate (EDA) were obtained from Aladdin. 4-Aminophenol and 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride were purchased from Macklin. Other chemicals and solvents were purchased from industry suppliers. All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without any further purification.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained on an Agilent Technologies Cary 60 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and zeta potential were carried out on a Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrometer and a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle size and zeta potential analyzer. The 1H NMR data was obtained in a Bruker AVANCE III spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were performed on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin high-resolution TEM at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV and a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. The fluorescence images in RAW 264.7 and HepG2 cells and living mice were recorded on a Leica TCS SP8 laser scanning confocal microscopy and a Biospace Lab Photonimager.
As shown in Scheme 1 , the NA-CQDs was synthesized by coupling CQDs, N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide, and 4-aminophenol.

The CQDs and N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide were synthesized according to the previous reports [38,39] as follows: To obtain the CQDs, CA (5.746 g, 27.3 mmol) and EDA (335 µL, 24.7 mmol) were dissolved in distilled water (50 mL). Then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200℃ for 5 h. The transparent solution was brown-black when the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature. The solution was subjected to dialysis (MW = 3500 Da) to obtain the CQDs. For the preparation of N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide, 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride (0.1385 g, 0.5 mmol) reacted with hydrazine hydrate (35 µL, 1.15 mmol) at 80℃ for 4 h in ethanol (30 mL), and the yellow precipitate was filtered and washed with ethanol with an 80 % yield. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6): 8.55 (d, J = 6.80 Hz, 1H), 8.51 (d, J = 5.6 Hz, 1H), 8.31 (d, J = 4.8 Hz, 1H), 8.20 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 8.00 (t, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 5.80 (s, 2H); FT-IR (KBr): 3338, 3240, 1609, 1586, 1503, 1459, 771 cm−1.

The CQDs (10 mg) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (0.0383 g, 0.2 mmol) were dissolved in distilled water (5 mL), and the solution was vigorously stirred in an ice bath for 30 min. N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide (0.0506 g, 0.2 mmol), N-hydroxysuccinimide (0.0230 g, 0.2 mmol), and DMF (5 mL) were added sequentially to the above solution and stirred vigorously at room temperature in the dark for 48 h. After purification by dialysis (MW = 3500 Da) for 72 h, the product (N-CQDs) was vacuum-dried at 50℃. The N-CQDs (40 mg) was dissolved in acetonitrile (5 mL), and then cesium carbonate (0.2630 g, 0.8 mmol) and 4-aminophenol (0.0327 g, 0.3 mmol) were added to the solution and stirred at 85℃ for 4 h. After purification by dialysis (MW = 3500 Da) for 72 h, the final product was vacuum dried at 50℃ to obtain the NA-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of HClO based on NA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NA-CQDs stock solution (10 mg·mL−1) was prepared with distilled water as solvent. In spectroscopic analysis, 40 µL of NA-CQDs stock solution was taken and diluted with 10 mM PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.0) to obtain a test solution of 0.08 mg·mL−1. The excitation wavelength of the probe was 395 nm, the emission wavelength was 457 nm, the slit width was 5.0/5.0 nm, and the scanning speed was 2400 nm/min.

The stock solutions of reactive oxygen and reactive nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) were 10 mM, including HClO, t-BuOOH, 1O2, H2O2, NO, NO, NO, O2 −, ONOO−, and ·OH. HClO was obtained by diluting sodium hypochlorite containing 7.5 % active chlorine with ultrapure water. HClO concentration was determined by measuring the absorbance at 292 nm and using the Beer-Lambert Law (A = εbc, ε = 350 M−1·cm−1) [40]. Commercial tert-butyl hydroperoxide, sodium nitrite, sodium nitrate, potassium superoxide, and 30 % hydrogen peroxide were used to dissolve to obtain t-BuOOH, NO, NO, O2 −, and H2O2 in ultrapure water. To determine H2O2 concentration, the absorbance at 240 nm was measured (ε = 43.6 M−1·cm−1). NO was prepared from sodium nitroprusside [41]. 1O2 was obtained through the reaction of NaClO with H2O2 (mole ratio = 1:2). ONOO− was generated by reacting hydrogen peroxide with sodium nitrite [42]. ‧OH was produced by mixing Fe2+ with excess H2O2 (the Fenton reaction) [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of HClO in real samples and test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of HClO in real samples, including apple, pineapple, and cucumber, tap water, and river water, was carried out by the spiked recovery experiments. 10 g of real samples (apple, pineapple, and cucumber) purchased from the local fruit mix were crushed and soaked in 20 mL of acetone for 36 h. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble substances and the pH was adjusted to 7.0. The filtrate was then diluted 100 times and mixed with the NA-CQDs stock solution. Similarly, the laboratory tap water and river water samples were obtained from the local pond and followed by filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane. Then HClO was added to the NA-CQDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra were measured.

The test strip experiment was performed using the NA-CQDs as the ink to write different patterns. A commercially available paper with no background fluorescence was chosen as the test strip material. The NA-CQDs solution was injected into the pen as the ink. Patterns were pre-drawn on the paper, and the strips were air-dried. The test strips were then immersed in different concentrations of HClO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity assay and bioimaging in living cells and nude mice
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of NA-CQDs was assessed in HepG2 cells by MTT assays. HepG2 cells were plated in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were then exposed to various concentrations of NA-CQDs ranging from 0.0025 to 0.16 mg·mL−1 for 2 h. After these cells were washed with PBS, 100 µL of MTT solution was added to each well. The medium was removed, and 150 µL DMSO was added after incubation for 4 h at 37℃. The absorbance at 570 nm was recorded by a microplate reader (Biotek).

The living cells, HepG2 and RAW 264.7 cells, were exposed to NA-CQDs (0.08 mg·mL−1 in Fetal Bovine Serum containing 1 % DMSO) for 2 h, followed by incubating with HClO (30 μΜ in PBS, pH = 7) for 30 min. Then, a Leica TCS SP8 laser scanning confocal microscopy was used to image RAW 264.7 and HepG2 cells with 405 nm excitation, and fluorescence emissions in the blue and green channels were collected in the ranges of 440–460 nm and 540–560 nm, respectively.

The male BALB/c nude mice (weight 25 g) were provided by Hangzhou Medical College (Hangzhou, China) for the visualization of the fluorescence images in vivo. Generally, the nude mice were divided into a control group and an experimental group. The mouse in the control group was injected with the nanoprobe NA-CQDs (0.16 mg·mL−1 in saline containing 1 % DMSO). The mouse in the experimental group was injected with the nanoprobe NA-CQDs and HClO (1 mM in saline). The subcutaneous injection was adopted as the injection method in this study. The control and the experimental groups were imaged at 5, 10, and 30 min.

The blue CQDs with rich –OH, –NH2, and –COOH groups was firstly synthesized by following a previous method using CA and EDA as precursors [38] (Scheme 1). By employing –COOH groups on the surface of CQDs, N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide with –NH2 groups could be coupled with CQDs through amidation reaction. Compared to CQDs, when N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide was introduced to the CQDs, the 1H NMR spectrum of the obtained N-CQDs appeared the characteristic naphthalene ring peaks from 7.97 ppm to 8.54 ppm [31] with another new peak at 5.80 ppm which was attributed to the proton of amide (Fig. S1), meaning N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide was grafted to the surface of the CQDs. Correspondingly, the N-CQDs showed the strongly reduced emission intensity at 477 nm compared to CQDs (Fig. S2). With further introduction of reignition group 4-aminophenol to N-CQDs, the fluorescence of NA-CQDs showed a 20 nm blue-shift from 477 nm to 457 nm. Meanwhile, the 1H NMR spectrum of the formed NA-CQDs emerged two double peaks at 6.69 ppm and 6.98 ppm, and a new broad proton peak at 5.26 ppm, where the former were assigned to protons of the benzene ring related to 4-aminophenol moiety and the latter to the amine proton [39]. In addition, the proton peak on the naphthalene ring of the N-CQDs at 8.30 ppm was shifted upfield to 6.86 ppm, indicating 4-aminophenol was modified to N-CQDs.

The CQDs, N-CQDs, and NA-CQDs were also confirmed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 a). The CQDs showed a broad absorption peak at 3442 cm−1, which belonged to –OH groups' stretching vibration [28]. The peaks at 3286 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 were attributed to –NH2 groups vibration (Fig. S3). The peaks at 1169 cm−1 and 1706 cm−1 indicated the presence of –OH, –NH2, and –COOH groups on the surface of CQDs [38]. Upon modification with N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide, the formed N-CQDs exhibited the new peak at 1653 cm−1 (C=O stretching vibration). This may be due to the formation of –CO–NH– groups after modification of N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide [44]. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks of the naphthalene ring of N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide at 1610 cm−1, 1588 cm−1, 1505 cm−1, and 1459 cm−1 and the new peak at 569 cm−1 which was attributed to aromatic C–Br stretching vibration also confirmed the successful functionalization with N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide. Meanwhile, compared with N-CQDs, the aromatic C–Br stretching vibration was not observed at 569 cm−1 and the new peak corresponding to C–O–C groups appeared at 1016 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of NA-CQDs. These results indicated the successful modification of the NA moiety to CQDs, which agreed with the 1H NMR analysis.

Further, the content of elements on the surface of CQDs, N-CQDs, and NA-CQDs were determined by XPS (Fig. 1b and Table S1). The CQDs, N-CQDs, and NA-CQDs were all composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements (Fig. 1b). Differ from CQDs and NA-CQDs, Br element was observed at 70.78 eV (3.99 %) [45] in XPS analysis of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, due to the reaction of −Br and –OH to form C–O–C bond, the XPS analysis of NA-CQDs did not contain Br element, verifying that the disappearance of the peak of aromatic C–Br stretching vibration in the FT-IR spectrum of NA-CQDs. Compared with CQDs, the carbon content of NA-CQDs increased from 68.16 % to 70.47 %. The nitrogen content increased from 11.6 % to 12.49 %, while the oxygen content decreased from 20.24 % to 17.04 % (Table S1). These results indicated the successful synthesis of CQDs, N-CQDs, and NA-CQDs.

The High-Resolution XPS spectrum of CQDs derived from C 1 s included the peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, 287.8 eV, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C=C (57.61 %), C–O/C–N (17.84 %), C=O (16.83 %), and COOH (7.72 %) bonds (Fig. 2 a). The C 1 s of NA-CQDs had the peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, 288.1 eV, and 289.7 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C=C (60.45 %), C–O/C–N (23.24 %), C=O (12.69 %), and COOH (3.62 %) bonds [46] (Fig. 2b). The percentage content of COOH bond decreased from 7.72 % to 3.62 %. The consumption of COOH groups may be due to the formation of –CO–NH– groups between CQDs and N-amino-4-bromonaphthalimide, corresponding to the peak at 1653 cm−1 (–CO–NH– groups) in the FT-IR spectrum. Meanwhile, the N 1 s of CQDs included two peaks at 398.3 eV and 400.2 eV, corresponding to the N–C=O (6.23 %) and –NH2 (93.77 %) bonds (Fig. 2c). For N 1 s of NA-CQDs, it derived two peaks at 398.3 eV and 400.2 eV, attributed to the N–C=O (7.95 %) and –NH2 (92.05 %) bonds [47] (Fig. 2d). The increment (6.23 % to 7.95 %) of N–C=O bond could also be due to the formation of –CO–NH– groups. The O 1 s of CQDs included two peaks at 531.5 eV and 533.0 eV, corresponding to the C=O (91.35 %) and C–O (8.65 %) bonds (Fig. 2e). For O 1 s of NA-CQDs, it derived two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.4 eV, attributed to the C=O (75.89 %) and C–O (24.11 %) bonds [46] (Fig. 2f). The increment (8.65 % to 24.11 %) of C-O bond might be due to the formation of C–O–C groups between N-CQDs and 4-aminophenol, verifying that the appearance of the new peak corresponding to C–O–C groups in the FT-IR spectrum of NA-CQDs.

The zeta potential and UV–vis absorption spectra were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The zeta potential of CQDs was −16.86 mV, which could be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CQDs' surface [48]. The introduction of NA to CQDs caused the zeta potential of NA-CQDs to increase to −9.62 mV (Fig. 3a), which might be the reason for the formation of –CO–NH– groups between the surface groups (–COOH) of CQDs portion and the amino groups (–NH2) of NA portion [15]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs, a distinct absorption peak at 335 nm was observed, which could be assigned to the n → π* transition of surface states [49]. After modification with NA, the absorption peak of NA-CQDs showed a red-shift with a wide absorption band from 320 nm to 430 nm (Fig. 3b), which may be due to the overlap between the red-shifted absorption peak of the CQDs moiety [50] and the absorption peak of the NA moiety [51].

Finally, the CQDs and NA-CQDs was investigated by TEM (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4, both CQDs and NA-CQDs were well dispersed with uniform morphologies and quasi-spherical structures. In addition, their high-resolution TEM images indicated the uniform crystal structure with the lattice spacing at about 0.24 nm (Fig. 4b and e). The average particle size of CQDs was 1.62 nm, while the average particle size of NA-CQDs was increased to 11.23 nm (Fig. 4c and f). The results indicated that the diameter of NA-CQDs increased after coupling of CQDs with NA [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of HClO based on NA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performance of NA-CQDs for HClO was studied through fluorescence titration experiments with different concentrations of HClO (Fig. 5 ).

When the nanoprobe NA-CQDs was excited at 395 nm, the NA-CQDs demonstrated the blue fluorescence at 457 nm. Adding HClO to the solution of NA-CQDs caused a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 457 nm. In comparison, the new NA-CQDs showed an excellent response to HClO, with a good linear relationship of y = 0.0504x + 0.3203 (R2 = 0.9903). The limit of detection for HClO was 99 nM (Fig. 5b) using the formula LOD = 3σ/slope [52]. Moreover, the NA-CQDs showed the characteristic fluorescence colorimetric change from blue to yellow under a 365 nm UV lamp with the increase of HClO concentration (Fig. 5c).

Different ROS/RNS species were also added to the solution of NA-CQDs to explore its selectivity (Fig. 6 a). Except for HClO, other ROS/RNS, including t-BuOOH, 1O2, H2O2, NO, NO, NO, O2 −, ONOO−, and ·OH, did not result in a significant fluorescence change of NA-CQDs. Correspondingly, as depicted in the inset in Fig. 6a, only HClO displayed a clear fluorescence color change in UV light (blue to yellow) when adding 30 μM of various analytes to the solution of NA-CQDs. These results presented that the NA-CQDs displayed good selectivity toward HClO.

The fluorescence response of NA-CQDs in the presence of HClO and other ROS/RNS was carried out to further study the anti-interference performance of NA-CQDs for HClO. Two sets of experiments were conducted as follows: other ROS/RNS species first were added individually to the NA-CQDs solution followed by adding HClO (Fig. 6b); HClO was added to the NA-CQDs solution, and then other ROS/RNS were added (Fig. 6c). When other ROS/RNS were present alone, the fluorescence intensity (I538/I457) of NA-CQDs had no obvious change. In contrast, when HClO was added, the fluorescence intensity of NA-CQDs increased significantly (Fig. 6b). When the NA-CQDs solution containing HClO was added to other ROS/RNS, the fluorescence intensity of NA-CQDs almost remained unchanged (Fig. 6c). These results indicated that the NA-CQDs possessed good anti-interference ability against other ROS/RNS species.

To improve the detection performance of NA-CQDs for HClO, the pH effect on NA-CQDs and its HClO detection was performed (Fig. 6d). The nanoprobe NA-CQDs showed low emission intensity compared to the emission in the presence of HClO, and its intensity had no significant change in the pH range of 2.0 to 7.0. When the pH was greater than 7.0, the intensity (I538/I457) of NA-CQDs increased slightly, which might result from the increased fluorescence intensity of the anionic form of the NA moiety [53]. Comparably, NA-CQDs exhibited ratiometric fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased from pH 2.0 to pH 5.0, and then it remained relatively stable from pH 5.0 to pH 12.0. The above results indicated that the optimal pH range for detecting HClO was 5.0 to 12.0. Due to the subsequent practical experiments for biological imaging [54], pH = 7.0 was chosen as the testing condition.

The reaction time of NA-CQDs for HClO was also studied. After adding HClO (10, 20, and 30 μM) to the NA-CQDs solution, the ratiometric fluorescence (I538/I457) increased rapidly and then remained stable after 20 s (Fig. 6e). Thus, the optimal conditions for detecting HClO were reaction time 20 s and pH = 7.0. The results indicated that the NA-CQDs exhibited low sensitivity to pH changes and could respond rapidly to HClO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanism of NA-CQDs for HClO
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the NA-CQDs showed a unique selectivity toward HClO with the ratiometric fluorescence change at the 457 and 538 nm emission bands and colorimetric response from blue to yellow (Fig. 7 a). Meanwhile, the introduction of HClO caused a new red-shifted absorption band at 460 nm accompanying the decreased absorbance at 375 nm (Fig. 7b), which might correspond to the oxidized species of NA moiety by removing p-aminophenylether (Fig. 7c).

In the absence of HClO, the emission of the NA moiety was switched off due to the presence of p-aminophenylether, which inhibited its ICT process as reported by [51]. Therefore, only CQDs’ blue fluorescence at 457 nm was observed. In the presence of HClO, HClO could oxidize NA moiety to form a new anionic part NA− through ether bond leakage of p-aminophenylether. Thus, the increased electronegativity triggered the ICT process of the naphthalimide moiety, and yellow fluorescence was observed. At the same time, CQDs could also be emitted at 395 nm and the emission was then absorbed by the new species NA−, as it had a new red-shifted absorption (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the FRET process took place [31,55,56], causing a decrease in fluorescence at 457 nm (Fig. 7c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of HClO in real samples and test strips
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of NA-CQDs, the NA-CQDs nanoprobe was evaluated by determining the recovery of the spiked HClO in real tap and river water samples, apple and pineapple fruits, and cucumber vegetables (Table 1 ). As listed in Table 1, whenever the real water samples, fruits, or vegetables, a good recovery of the spiked HClO was found for five different samples with the RSD in the range of 1.0 % to 5.0 %, and the recovery of HClO was between 93.7 % and 106.0 %, indicating that the nanoprobe exhibited the good ability to detect HClO in real samples.

A visual test strip detection based on the NA-CQDs was investigated for quick practical application with the naked eye (Fig. 8 ). Two patterns, a four-leaf clover and a butterfly were designed and drawn with a pen using the NA-CQDs as the ink. The original test strips showed no fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, but the patterns on the test strips showed blue fluorescence. As the HClO’s amount was increased, the blue patterns gradually faded and yellow fluorescence developed, and hence a yellow four-leaf clover and a yellow butterfly were observed on the test strips with the naked eye. It revealed that the NA-CQDs-based test strips could be used to visualize and detect HClO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity assay and bioimaging in living cells and nude mice
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity assay was first carried out, as shown in Fig. S4. Then, bioimaging experiments of NA-CQDs in living cells and nude mice were performed, and the results are shown in Figs. 9-10
.

The cytotoxicity of NA-CQDs was evaluated using MTT assays. After exposure to various concentrations of NA-CQDs ranging from 0.0025 to 0.16 mg·mL−1 for 2 h, the viability of HepG2 cells was measured. More than 79 % of the cells remained alive, which showed that NA-CQDs had low toxicity to HepG2 cells (Fig. S4).

Fluorescence images were performed in the living RAW 264.7 (Fig. 9a and b) and HepG2 cells (Fig. 9c and d). As shown in Fig. 9a and c, after staining with 0.08 mg·mL−1 of NA-CQDs for 2 h, clear fluorescence images were observed in the living cells in the blue channel (440–460 nm), while no obvious fluorescence in the living cells was collected out in the green channel (540–560 nm), which was consistent with the probe NA-CQDs’ fluorescence characteristic of emission at 457 nm. When further incubated with HClO in PBS solution for 30 min (Fig. 9b and d), the fluorescence in the blue channel was decreased and significant fluorescence intensity was recorded in the green channel, suggesting that the probe NA-CQDs could effectively sense HClO in living cells.

For the fluorescence imaging of living mice, the results of the control and an experimental group are shown in Fig. 10. The control group was injected 0.16 mg·mL−1 of NA-CQDs into the belly of the mouse, and then the fluorescent imaging was conducted with the time interval in 5 min. As shown in Fig. 10a, the fluorescence of the probe NA-CQDs was observed at 440–470 nm and it remained relatively stable over the time range of the fluorescence imaging (Fig. 10a). Comparably, the fluorescence on the mouse in the experiment group injected with the nanoprobe NA-CQDs and HClO (1 mM in saline) gradually decreased in the intensity from 5 min to 30 min in the range of 440 to 470 nm (Fig. S5), but gradually increased in the range of 530 to 557 nm while no fluorescence images were available for the control group in the range of 530 to 557 nm (Fig. 10b), meaning that HClO in living mice was detected by the nanoprobe NA-CQDs. These results revealed that the NA-CQDs exhibited potential proficiency in detecting HClO within living organisms.

A ratiometric nanoprobe (NA-CQDs) based on naphthalimide modified on the surface of carbon quantum dots was designed and synthesized. The CQDs was prepared using a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The average particle size of CQDs was 1.62 nm and well dispersed with uniform morphologies and quasi-spherical structures. Then naphthalimide was modified carbon quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescence sensing HClO. In the presence of HClO, the emission intensity of the NA-CQDs solution gradually decreased at 457 nm and rapidly increased at 538 nm with a change in the fluorescence color from blue to yellow. The results indicated that the NA-CQDs responded linearly to the HClO concentration up to 32 μM with a limit detection of 99 nM. Compared with the other CQDs-based nanoprobes (Table 2 ), the NA-CQDs nanoprobe showed a fast response to HClO in 20 s. Its relatively low LOD and built-in correction function of ratiometric detection would also be beneficial for potential environmental sensing and biosensing in living organisms in a variety of applications, making this method particularly advantageous. In subsequent studies, it is crucial to develop high-sensitivity and low-cost ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on CQDs for application in biological imaging and biomedicine.
